old_claim.note: Global Exploration provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Darwin traced the problem to observations made as naturalist aboard H.M.S. Beagle and used geographical distribution as a core evidentiary line.

source_shape.source_locator: 1859 first-edition text: Darwin says that, while naturalist aboard H.M.S. Beagle, facts about South American distribution and geological relations threw light on species origins; chapters 11-12 address geographical distribution.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Darwin connects the origin problem to Beagle voyage observations and then treats geographical distribution as a major evidence line.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports an enabling edge because oceanic exploration generated the field observations and biogeographic comparisons that fed the 1859 theory.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: theory_of_evolution_darwin remains enabled by early modern/global exploration, now with primary-source evidence.
would_reject_if: The Darwin node is later narrowed to only laboratory heredity or domestic-breeding analogies.
A stronger source shows Darwin's Beagle observations and geographical distribution evidence were incidental to the 1859 argument.
The exploration_global node is narrowed away from oceanic voyage-based natural-history observation.
short_reason: Darwin's Beagle observations and geographical distribution chapters tie the theory to oceanic exploration evidence.
